questions.question: In East Donegal Co-Operative Livestock Mart Ltd v Attorney General [1970] IR 317, Walsh J articulated the 'double construction rule'. Which of the following most precisely states that rule?

options.A: Where a statute is capable of two constructions — one constitutional and one unconstitutional — the court must declare the statute unconstitutional to vindicate the Constitution's supremacy
options.B: Where a statutory provision is reasonably capable of a construction that is constitutional, the court will adopt that construction and uphold the provision rather than declare it invalid
options.C: Where the Oireachtas has enacted legislation, the court presumes both the procedure of enactment and the substance of the measure are constitutionally valid
options.D: Where two statutes conflict, the court must adopt a construction of both that renders them consistent with each other and with the Constitution

questions.explanation: The double construction rule from East Donegal provides that where a statutory provision is susceptible of two constructions — one consistent with and one repugnant to the Constitution — the court will adopt the constitutional construction and uphold the statute. This flows from the presumption of constitutionality: the courts presume the Oireachtas intended to legislate consistently with the Constitution, so they strain to find a valid reading before declaring invalidity. Option C conflates the double construction rule with the broader presumption of constitutionality. Option D describes harmonious statutory interpretation, not the rule in East Donegal.

questions.question: In Attorney General v Ryan's Car Hire Ltd [1965] IR 642, the Supreme Court considered how to resolve a conflict between the Irish and English texts of the Constitution. Which of the following most accurately states the principle applied?

options.A: The court must apply a purposive approach, selecting whichever text best gives effect to the framers' demonstrated legislative intent
options.B: The court must prefer the English text as the more precisely drafted version, given that the Constitution was negotiated and drafted primarily in English
options.C: Where the Irish and English texts diverge, the Irish text (Bunreacht na hEireann) prevails by virtue of Article 25.5.4 degrees, and the court applied a literal reading of that text
options.D: Both texts are of equal authority; the court must adopt the construction that gives greater protection to the individual's constitutional rights

questions.explanation: Article 25.5.4 degrees provides that in any case of conflict between the Irish and English texts of the Constitution, the Irish text shall prevail. In Ryan's Car Hire, the Supreme Court applied this provision literally: where the two texts diverged, the Irish text governed. This case is the leading authority on the primacy of the Irish text. Option B is incorrect as it inverts the constitutional position. Option D is incorrect as the texts are not of equal authority — the Irish text is the authoritative version.

questions.question: In McGee v Attorney General [1974] IR 284, Walsh J delivered a judgment that has become central to discussions of natural law and constitutional interpretation. Which of the following most accurately captures Walsh J's approach to the nature of constitutional rights?

options.A: Walsh J held that constitutional rights are exhaustively enumerated in the text and courts have no power to imply rights not expressly stated
options.B: Walsh J held that rights acknowledged in Articles 40 to 44 are natural rights antecedent to positive law, which the Constitution recognises rather than creates
options.C: Walsh J held that constitutional rights derive exclusively from Catholic social teaching and that the courts must apply Papal encyclicals as interpretive guides
options.D: Walsh J held that the Constitution should be interpreted to give effect to the subjective intentions of its principal drafter, Eamon de Valera

questions.explanation: In McGee, Walsh J articulated a natural law approach: rights enumerated in Articles 40 to 44 are not created by the Constitution but are pre-existing natural rights which the State and the Constitution acknowledge and guarantee. He stated that 'natural rights... are antecedent to positive law'. This underpins the doctrine of unenumerated rights: because rights pre-exist the Constitution, the list in Articles 40 to 44 is not exhaustive. Option C overstates the religious dimension — Walsh J invoked natural law theory, not Papal authority as such. Option A is the opposite of Walsh J's approach. Option D confuses natural law interpretation with original intent theory.

questions.question: Roche v Roche [2010] IESC 10 raised the question of whether frozen embryos constituted 'unborn' life within Article 40.3.3 degrees. The Supreme Court's treatment of natural law in that case is significant for constitutional interpretation methodology. Which of the following most accurately describes the court's approach?

options.A: The Supreme Court unanimously applied the natural law approach from McGee and held that frozen embryos enjoy full constitutional protection under Article 40.3.3 degrees
options.B: The Supreme Court unanimously rejected natural law as a basis for constitutional interpretation, holding that rights derive solely from the constitutional text
options.C: A majority of the Supreme Court declined to apply natural law principles to resolve the question, preferring a textual interpretation and signalling a retreat from the natural law approach
options.D: The Supreme Court applied a purposive approach and held that the framers' intentions in 1937 were decisive, regardless of subsequent developments in natural law thinking

questions.explanation: Roche v Roche [2010] represents a significant retreat from the natural law approach. A majority of the Supreme Court declined to invoke natural law to determine the status of frozen embryos and instead adopted a more textual approach, leaving the matter to the legislature. Several judges expressed reservations about the court's earlier embrace of natural law as an interpretive source. This contrasts with McGee and Norris v AG [1984] where natural law featured prominently. The case signals that Irish constitutional law has moved toward a more positivist and textual methodology, particularly for sensitive socio-ethical questions.

questions.question: The presumption of constitutionality, as elaborated in Pigs Marketing Board v Donnelly [1939] IR 413 and East Donegal Co-Operative [1970] IR 317, has several components. Which of the following correctly identifies both the standard trigger for the presumption AND one significant limitation on its application?

options.A: Trigger: the Act was enacted after 1937; Limitation: the presumption does not apply where the plaintiff adduces clear evidence of unconstitutional intent by the Oireachtas
options.B: Trigger: the Act post-dates the enactment of the Constitution; Limitation: the presumption is rebutted where the Act on its face and without more is plainly unconstitutional
options.C: Trigger: the Act was passed unanimously by both Houses; Limitation: the presumption does not apply to Acts that have not been referred to the Supreme Court under Article 26
options.D: Trigger: the Act was signed by the President without an Article 26 reference; Limitation: the presumption is irrebuttable once the President has signed the Bill into law

questions.explanation: The presumption of constitutionality applies to post-1937 Acts of the Oireachtas: because the Oireachtas is constitutionally bound (Art. 15.4) to enact only constitutional legislation, a post-1937 Act is presumed constitutionally valid. The presumption is rebuttable — in East Donegal, Walsh J acknowledged it does not apply where the Act is 'on its face and without more' plainly repugnant to the Constitution. Option A's limitation (evidence of unconstitutional intent) mischaracterises the standard. Option D is incorrect: Article 26 references produce an irrebuttable presumption only for the specific provisions referred (Art. 34.3.2 degrees). Pre-1937 Acts carry no presumption of constitutionality at all.

questions.question: The Supreme Court in DPP v O'Shea [1982] IR 384 split sharply on how to interpret 'trial by jury' under Article 38.5 of the Constitution. Which of the following best describes the significance of this case for constitutional interpretation methodology?

options.A: The case established that the Constitution must always be interpreted in light of contemporary social values rather than the meaning of words at the time of enactment
options.B: The majority applied an approach closer to original meaning, interpreting Article 38.5 by reference to how trial by jury operated in 1937, while the minority favoured a broader evolving interpretation
options.C: The majority held that the Constitution must be interpreted harmoniously with other provisions and the Irish text, concluding that jury unanimity was constitutionally required
options.D: The court unanimously adopted a purposive approach, holding that the right to trial by jury protected a substantive right to individual juror assent that could not be abrogated by statute

questions.explanation: DPP v O'Shea [1982] exposed a fundamental methodological division in the Supreme Court. The majority (Griffin, Hederman, O'Higgins CJ) applied an approach closer to original meaning, interpreting 'trial by jury' as it operated historically in 1937, concluding the Constitution did not require jury unanimity since majority verdicts were known at that time. The minority (Walsh, Henchy JJ) favoured a broader, more dynamic reading of constitutional rights. This case illustrates that the Irish Supreme Court has not settled on any single interpretive methodology — different judges apply literal, purposive, natural law, or historical approaches, producing divergent outcomes on the same text.

questions.question: Heaney v Ireland [1994] 3 IR 593 (Costello J, High Court) established a test for determining whether a restriction on a constitutional right is permissible. Which of the following correctly states that test?

options.A: A restriction on a constitutional right is permissible only if it is expressly authorised by a specific provision of the Constitution itself
options.B: A restriction on a constitutional right is permissible only if the right restricted is not a 'personal right' within the meaning of Article 40.3
options.C: The restriction must be rationally connected to a legitimate objective, impair the right as little as reasonably possible, and its effects must be proportionate to the objective — the proportionality test
options.D: A restriction is permissible if a majority of the Oireachtas has determined it is necessary for the common good under the Directive Principles of Social Policy in Article 45

questions.explanation: In Heaney v Ireland, Costello J articulated the 'proportionality test': (1) the objective must be sufficiently important to warrant limiting a fundamental right; (2) the restriction must be rationally connected to the objective and not arbitrary; (3) the restriction must impair the right as little as reasonably possible; and (4) the effects on the right must be proportionate to the objective. Derived in part from Canadian Charter jurisprudence (R v Oakes [1986] SCC), this test has been widely applied in Irish constitutional cases involving rights limitations. It represents the dominant modern methodology for assessing restrictions on constitutional rights and has been applied in cases ranging from the right to silence to freedom of association.

questions.question: The 'harmonious interpretation' principle requires different constitutional provisions to be read together rather than in isolation. In which of the following scenarios does this principle most directly operate?

options.A: Where a post-1937 statute conflicts with the Constitution, the court reads the statute harmoniously with the Constitution and, if necessary, reads down the statute
options.B: Where two constitutional provisions appear to conflict, the court reads them together to give effect to both, rather than treating one as absolutely overriding the other
options.C: Where a litigant seeks to enforce a right under both the Constitution and the ECHR, the court applies both instruments harmoniously to maximise protection
options.D: Where the Irish and English constitutional texts diverge, the court applies harmonious interpretation to resolve the ambiguity before invoking Article 25.5.4 degrees

questions.explanation: Harmonious interpretation requires the various provisions of the Constitution to be read as parts of an integrated, coherent whole. Where two constitutional provisions appear to conflict, the court should not simply subordinate one to the other but should seek a reading that gives full effect to both. This was applied in cases balancing property rights (Art. 43) against social justice (Art. 45) and in cases involving press freedom (Art. 40.6.1 degrees(i)) against the right to a fair trial. Option A describes reading down of statutes — a related but distinct doctrine. Option D misidentifies when Art. 25.5.4 degrees applies: that provision is invoked where the Irish and English texts are irreconcilable, not to avoid the need to invoke it.

questions.question: In Norris v Attorney General [1984] IR 36, the Supreme Court upheld legislation criminalising homosexual acts between consenting adult males. The majority's reasoning is significant for its interpretive methodology. Which of the following most accurately captures the approach applied by the majority?

options.A: The majority applied a progressive 'living document' approach, holding that constitutional rights must evolve with changing social norms, but concluded the right to privacy did not extend that far
options.B: The majority applied natural law principles, holding that the right to privacy does not extend to conduct deemed contrary to the natural moral law, and read the Constitution in light of its Christian ethos
options.C: The majority applied the double construction rule and upheld the legislation as a legitimate regulation of the common good under Article 40.3
options.D: The majority adopted a purely textual approach, holding that the explicit terms of Article 40.6.1 degrees did not protect the conduct in question

questions.explanation: The majority in Norris (O'Higgins CJ, Finlay P, Griffin J) invoked the Christian and natural law framework of the Constitution — particularly the Preamble's invocation of the Most Holy Trinity and the Constitution's acknowledgement of God — to hold that the right to marital privacy recognised in McGee v AG [1974] did not extend to homosexual conduct, characterised as contrary to natural and Christian morality. This represents a significant use of natural law and religious ethos in constitutional interpretation. The decision was effectively overturned in practice by the European Court of Human Rights in Norris v Ireland (1988) 13 EHRR 186 and the law was reformed by the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 1993. Norris is now a cautionary example of the dangers of natural law reasoning producing discriminatory outcomes.

questions.question: The concept of unenumerated rights in Irish constitutional law is closely connected to which interpretive methodology, and which Article provides the principal textual basis for recognising such rights?

options.A: The presumption of constitutionality; Article 15.4 — which implicitly acknowledges rights beyond those expressly stated
options.B: Natural law and natural rights theory; Article 40.3.1 degrees — which refers to the State's duty to protect 'personal rights' without exhaustively defining them
options.C: The double construction rule; Article 40.1 — which establishes equality before the law as the foundation from which all other rights derive
options.D: The purposive approach; Article 29.2 — which obliges Ireland to adhere to principles of international human rights law, incorporating UDHR rights by implication

questions.explanation: Unenumerated rights derive from Article 40.3.1 degrees, which obliges the State to respect and defend the 'personal rights of the citizen' — without specifying what those rights are. This deliberately open-textured provision has been interpreted, particularly through the natural law/natural rights methodology of Walsh J in McGee v AG [1974] and Ryan v AG [1965] (Kenny J, High Court), to permit the courts to recognise constitutional rights not explicitly listed in Articles 40 to 44. Examples include rights to bodily integrity (Ryan v AG), privacy (McGee), travel (State (M) v Minister for Foreign Affairs [1979]), and earned livelihood (Murtagh Properties v Cleary [1972]). Option B correctly pairs the methodology (natural law) with the textual basis (Art. 40.3.1 degrees).
